region_groups.name: Mountain West
region_groups.description: Interior West and Southwest basins—high elevation, aridity, and rapid diurnal shifts shape short mountain summers and long-storing root crops.

regions.name: Montana
regions.description: Montana’s cold winters and dry summers favor wheat, barley, pulses, and summer farmers’ market produce on valley bottoms.

regions.name: Wyoming
regions.description: Wyoming agriculture centers on beef, hay, and sugar beets—garden produce peaks briefly at high altitude.

regions.name: Colorado
regions.description: Colorado melds high-country ranching with Front Range orchards, Palisade peaches, Olathe sweet corn, and greenhouse supply near cities.

regions.name: New Mexico
regions.description: New Mexico’s chiles, pecans, onions, and pinto beans reflect desert heat and Rio Grande irrigation—frost dates vary sharply by elevation.

regions.name: Utah
regions.description: Utah’s mountain valleys grow tart cherries, apples, and summer squash; red-rock south pushes warmer-season crops.

regions.name: Arizona
regions.description: Arizona combines high-desert cattle country with Yuma’s winter vegetable capital and central desert citrus and dates.

regions.name: Nevada
regions.description: Nevada is overwhelmingly arid—alfalfa, cattle, and greenhouse or high-desert growers supply local summer produce near urban centers.
